EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure dominated the western United States for several days. This resulted in scorching heat and dry weather across the lower elevations of eastern Utah. Conditions were exceptionally dry which translated to single digit humidity at the surface. Record breaking high temperatures occurred through the period with overnight lows only dropping into the low to mid to upper 60s and 70s in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures of 101 to 106 degrees were reported by various automated weather stations and observers each day through the period. Additionally, overnight lows ranged from 78 to 79 degrees on the morning of the 17th and 18th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of June as exceptional (D4) conditions persisted across all but the northern part of the zone where extreme (D3) conditions continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged monsoon pattern persisted across much of the region the last couple of weeks of July. This led to cooler and unsettled conditions and brought some much needed precipitation to eastern Utah. As a result, drought conditions improved by one or more categories across some southern zones by the end of the month. However, conditions remained unchanged or worsened slightly further north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of July as exceptional (D4) conditions persisted across all but the northern part of the zone where extreme (D3) conditions continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought heavy snow to northern Utah, including historic avalanche conditions in Little Cottonwood Canyon, and even heavy snow in the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports included 58 inches at Alta Ski Area, 50 inches at Snowbird, 31 inches at Big Cottonwood Spruces, 30 inches at Park City Mountain Resort, and 22 inches at SunCrest. In addition, the Town of Alta was placed under interlodge for 60 consecutive hours due to extreme avalanche conditions, beating the record setting 52 hours of interlodge set in February of 2020. State Route 210 was closed at 11 p.m. on Monday, February 15, and remained closed until the afternoon of Thursday, February 18. Both Alta Ski Area and Snowbird were closed for two straight days (Tuesday, February 16 and Wednesday, February 17). A reported 159 avalanches of Size 2 or larger were detected during the event, with 34 of those avalanches impacting state Route 210. In addition, peak wind gusts of 87 mph and 74 mph were recorded at Hidden Peak and Mount Baldy, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought heavy snow to northern Utah, including historic avalanche conditions in Little Cottonwood Canyon, and even heavy snow in the Salt Lake Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy snow was reported across the Salt Lake Valley, including 19 inches in Cottonwood Heights, 16 inches in Olympus Cove, 14.5 inches in South Salt Lake, 14 inches in the Salt Lake City Avenues, 13.2 inches at Salt Lake City International Airport, 12 inches in Sugarhouse, 11 inches in Taylorsville, and 8 inches in West Jordan. Tooele Valley reports included 6 inches in both Tooele and Stansbury Park. Impacts included many schools closed or delayed starts, 219 crashes, COVID-19 testing and vaccine appointment canceled or delayed, and aviation impacts.  At Salt Lake City International Airport, 17 departing flights were cancelled, 71 departing flights were delayed, 19 arriving flights were canceled, and 34 arriving flights were delayed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports included 13 inches at Powder Mountain and in the Logan Mountains, and 12 inches at Snowbasin Resort. In addition, the Tony Grove SNOTEL  recorded 2 inches of snow water equivalent, or approximately 22 inches of snow, while the Ben Lomond Peak SNOTEL recorded 1.9 inches of snow water equivalent, or approximately 21 inches of snow.  The Farmington Peak SNOTEL recorded 1.7 inches of snow water equivalent, or approximately 19 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backcountry avalanche at Wilson Glade in Millcreek Canyon, just outside of Salt Lake City, caught and fully buried six people, killing four within the group.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Forest Service Utah Avalanche Center reported that two groups, totaling eight people, were backcountry skiing at Wilson Glade in Millcreek Canyon, when an avalanche was triggered. The avalanche caught and buried six of the people, leading to four fatalities, while two survived, one with injuries. The avalanche occurred in terrain that was rated for High avalanche danger that day. The weather the day of the avalanche was sunny and fair. However, two storm systems the week prior to the avalanche brought roughly 20 inches of snow and winds 15-30 mph, with gusts up to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms generated gusty outflow winds, heavy rain and frequent lightning across eastern Utah on June 24th. Two people were struck by lightning while recreating in Dead Horse State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were struck by lightning while recreating on the West Rim Trail at Dead Horse State Park. Crews found both individuals in critical but stable condition before they were transported to Moab Regional Hospital for care.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorm wind gusts occurred near Deer Creek Dam in northern Utah and near Hogan Pass in central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 65 mph was recorded by the U.S. Highway 189 at Deer Creek Dam sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorm wind gusts occurred near Deer Creek Dam in northern Utah and near Hogan Pass in central Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season heat wave brought unusually hot temperatures across populated portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unusually early heat wave developed, with daily high temperatures up to 105 degrees at St. George, and warm overnight temperatures in the 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season heat wave brought unusually hot temperatures across populated portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unusually early heat wave developed, including a daily high temperature of 102 degrees at Big Water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessively hot temperatures included a daytime of 104 degrees at Tooele, with a nighttime low of only 80 degrees, and a daily average temperature nearly 24 degrees above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat conditions included a daily high temperature of 106 degrees at Ogden, and nighttime lows in the 70s. This pushed daily temperature average to 20 degrees above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On June 15, the temperature at Salt Lake City International Airport reached 107 degrees, tying the all time highest temperature ever recorded at the site. This combined with nighttime lows in the 70s, resulted in daily average temperatures of 20 degrees above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures up to 105 degrees were recorded at the Provo BYU COOP station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temperature at the Myton COOP station reached 101 degrees for three days in a row.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temperature reached at least 107 degrees five days in a row at Hanksville, including a 109 degree reading on one day. In addition, low temperatures remained in the 70s for the second half of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures up to 114 degrees were recorded, including many days above 105 degrees. Nighttime low temperatures remained in the 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reached 100 degrees or warmer at Kanab during the period, including a day up to 106 degrees.  Overnight lows were in the 70s some nights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of high temperatures over 100 degrees, with low temperatures above 70, occurred at Big Water.  Daily high temperatures including 107, recorded twice.  Overnight lows temperatures were as warm as 84 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temperature at Price reached 101 degrees two days in a row, followed by a night with the low temperature in the 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred across portions of the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument, in Zion National Park, and in the town of Hilldale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was observed at the state Route 12 overpass of the Escalante River. Additional reports of flash flooding were observed upstream, including in Escalante River Canyon and at Death Hollow. The Escalante River gauge climbed from no flow to 400 CFS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred across portions of the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument, in Zion National Park, and in the town of Hilldale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hildale Fire Department personnel reported multiple road closures in town, multiple flooded residences, and several stranded motorists due to flash flooding in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough dug into the Great Basin before lifting across eastern Utah. Southwest flow favored the La Sal and Abajo Mountains throughout much of the event with moderate snow falling across the aforementioned areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the La Sal Mountains with 8 to 12 inches in the Abajos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across eastern Utah which resulted in a tight wind gradient aloft. These stronger winds mixed down to the surface with some gusts meeting high wind criteria over the Eastern Uinta Basin and the Arches and Grand Flat areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by the Vernal Citizen Weather Observer Program (CWOP) automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across eastern Utah which resulted in a tight wind gradient aloft. These stronger winds mixed down to the surface with some gusts meeting high wind criteria over the Eastern Uinta Basin and the Arches and Grand Flat areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the Bryson Canyon automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and slow moving area of low pressure impacted the region for several days. As the low slowly lifted south of the Four Corners, significant moisture wrapped around the low and advected into the area, favoring the southern mountains at first and then favoring the northern mountains as flow turned to the north. Heavy snow fell in the mountains with some ranges estimating 30 to 40 inches of snow. Winds gusted up to 55 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 20 to 30 inches of snow fell across the higher elevations with locally higher amounts of up to 43 inches estimated at the Hickerson Park SNOTEL site. The CoCoRaHS observer in Dutch John measured almost 12 inches of snow. Winds gusted from 45 to 55 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and slow moving area of low pressure impacted the region for several days. As the low slowly lifted south of the Four Corners, significant moisture wrapped around the low and advected into the area, favoring the southern mountains at first and then favoring the northern mountains as flow turned to the north. Heavy snow fell in the mountains with some ranges estimating 30 to 40 inches of snow. Winds gusted up to 55 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated two feet of snow fell at the East Willow Creek SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 45 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong and slow moving area of low pressure impacted the region for several days. Heavy snow fell in the mountains with some ranges estimating 30 to 40 inches of snow. Winds gusted up to 55 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 15 inches of snow fell across the Abajo Mountains with 20 to 35 inches measured in the La Sals. The Cooperative Observer in the Monticello area received 6 inches of snow. Winds gusted from 45 to 55 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred across portions of the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument, in Zion National Park, and in the town of Hilldale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding occurred from Wire Pass to the mouth of Buckskin Gulch. Several hikers were stranded between Wire Pass and the entrance to Buckskin Gulch, with muddy water levels rising to waist deep. Another group was trapped within Buckskin Gulch overnight due to Flash Flooding. The group was able to climb onto narrow ledges above the flood waters, where they remained overnight, then evacuated the next day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two-day stretch of abnormally hot temperatures occurred, with daily average temperatures running as high as 16 degrees above average.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread heatwave brought several days of excessively hot temperatures across the state.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season heat wave brought unusually hot temperatures across populated portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unusually early heat wave developed, including the earliest 100 degree day on record, June 4, occurring at Salt Lake City International Airport.  Daily average temperatures for the period were 15 to 16 degrees above average. Cooling centers were opened in Salt Lake County and Utah Department of Transportation road crews adjusted working hours to avoid peak heating hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred across portions of the Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument, in Zion National Park, and in the town of Hilldale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred from the entrance of Zion National Park, across the eastern portions of the park. State Route 9 within the park closed due to flooding and debris on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure originating over southern California lifted across the Desert Southwest. Strong forcing aloft and ample moisture produced moderate snow over southeast Utah, including both the higher elevations and the lower valleys. Winds in the mountains gusted to 47 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 7 inches of snow fell in the Abajos with around 4 inches across the La Sal Mountains. The Cooperative Observer in Monticello measured 5.5 inches of snow. Winds gusted up to 47 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive Pacific trough trekked across eastern Utah. Mild southwest flow ahead of the storm kept temperatures well above normal before the trough moved through during the overnight hours. Rain fell in the valleys before changing over the snow. Moderate snowfall totals were reported over the La Sal and Abajo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 13 inches of snow fell across the La Sal Mountains with 4 to 6 inches in the Abajos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture remained trapped underneath the inversions across northeast Utah which led to the development of dense fog in the Eastern Uinta Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Eastern Uinta Basin including at the Vernal Regional Airport as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level flow shifted to the west-southwest and then the southwest ahead of an approaching Pacific storm. The storm split into two systems - a closed low tracking over the Desert Southwest and a northern wave which trekked across eastern Utah on the night of the 22nd. A secondary wave on the backside of the trough moved over the southern half of the forecast area on the 24th. This long duration storm brought moderate snow to the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area.

A secondary wave on the backside of the trough moved over the southern half of the forecast area on the 24th. This long duration storm brought moderate snow to the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally a foot of snow fell in the La Sal Mountains with 14 to 17 inches of snow estimated across the Abajos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifted across the Four Corners area. Warm southwest flow out ahead of the system kept temperatures warm in the valleys with some areas receiving rain before all precipitation changed to snow as a strong cold front moved through. Moderate to heavy snow fell in the La Sal and Abajo Mountains as well as in the Monticello area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 16 inches of snow fell across the La Sal and Abajo Mountains with 11 inches of snow measured in the Monticello area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near to slightly below normal temperatures prevailed across much of eastern Utah during the month of February. Additionally, wetter than normal conditions occurred across many areas, though pockets of below normal precipitation prevailed in some spots. Regardless, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of the region with the extreme to exceptional category dominating many areas through the end of the month. However, there was some improvement in drought category in portions of southeast Utah towards the end of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) drought persisted across all but the northern part of the zone through the end of the month. Up north, conditions improved to the extreme (D3) category in late February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front progressed across the region. As a result, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern Utah. Moderate instability produced some stronger thunderstorms that generated severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough progressed across the region. As a result, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern Utah. However, dry low levels led to gusty winds rather than heavy wetting rains with convection. Some storms generated severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport.

Train tracks were determined to be washed out at a small bridge crossing over a normally dry wash. In addition, emergency responders were washed off of the road to the crash site by the flood waters, delaying response to the injured train operators.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active monsoon surge brought thunderstorms producing heavy rain, damaging wind gusts, and hail across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several lane on Interstate 15 near Bountiful were closed due to standing water. Hill Field Road near Hill Air Force Base was flooded.  Basement flooding occurred in Centerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active monsoon surge brought thunderstorms producing heavy rain, damaging wind gusts, and hail across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant flood wave carrying debris moved down Bear Creek Canyon, sweeping away mining vehicles at the Gentry Mountain Mine. Several miners were caught in the flood waters, with one miner able to save himself by clinging to a tree a quarter mile down the drainage. Another miner was not able to escape the flood waters and was found deceased 6 miles downstream. Another Mine car was flipped 8 times in the flood waters, but the miners were able to kick out the windows and escape. Significant infrastructure damage occurred at the Mine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system which was associated with a prolonged atmospheric river event moved across eastern Utah. The abundance of moisture associated with this system paired with the strong cold front resulted in widespread snow showers in the higher elevations. Moderate snow fell in the Eastern Uinta Mountains, generally above 9,500 feet. Additionally, gusty winds accompanied the cold front with gusts up to 60 mph recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 60 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of disturbances moved across eastern Utah during the month of October. This led to areas of above normal precipitation for the month which resulted in some improvement of the drought across the region, most notably across the northern zones.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across much of the zone during the month of October as the extreme (D3) drought was trimmed back to include just the far western part of the zone and improved to the severe (D2) category over the central part. Meanwhile, the severe drought improved to the moderate (D1) category across the far eastern portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough progressed across the region as subtropical moisture streamed in from the south. As a result, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across eastern Utah. Moderate instability produced some stronger thunderstorms that generated severe wind gusts as well as heavy rain which led to isolated debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 62 MPH was measured by the automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike most likely ignited a wildfire near Moonwater and Hill Creeks in northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Desolation Fire most likely started due to lightning in mid August near Moonwater and Hill Creeks on tribal land in northeast Utah. The fire grew to 211 acres before being contained a couple of weeks later.

The culverts were not big enough to hold water. Water, mud and debris went over the road and came into some basements a couple inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure originating from the Pacific Northwest dropped into the Great Basin. As this feature slowly lifted across the region it tapped into a stream of monsoon moisture from the south. The upper level lift paired with the abundance of moisture resulted in widespread showers and thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized flooding and debris flows in Grand County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred in the Salt Wash that crosses Delicate Arch Road in Arches National Park. 3 to 6 inches of mud was deposited across the road and a few medium to large size boulders were moved.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood resulted in the temporary closure of Hunter Canyon Campground along the Kane Creek Road near Moab. The local Bureau of Land Management office had pictures of Kane Springs Creek running high and water spilling into the campground.

As a result, drought conditions improved by one or more categories across some zones by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across much of the zone through the month of August. Exceptional (D4) conditions improved to the extreme (D3) category across all but the northeast portion of the zone where the exceptional drought continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture advected into the region during the month of August. This led to cooler and unsettled conditions and brought some much needed precipitation to eastern Utah. As a result, drought conditions improved by one or more categories across some zones by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought improved across some of the zone through the month of August. The extreme (D3) drought improved to the severe (D2) category across the far northwest portion of the zone with the exceptional (D4) drought improving to the extreme category over the far southwest portion. The extreme drought remained unchanged elsewhere except over the southeast portion of the zone where the exceptional drought continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought significant snowfall to portions of Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow reports included 12 inches in Providence, 11 inches in Logan, and 8 inches in Hyrum. Heavy wet snow brought down numerous fully leafed trees and limbs, caused property damage, knocked out power to thousands of customers, and caused the delay and cancellation of multiple schools.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow amounts included 11.9 inches in Cedar City and 10 inches in New Harmony. Heavy wet snow on fully leafed trees brought down numerous tree limbs in Cedar City and New Harmony.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system lifted across eastern Utah and brought with it an abundance of Pacific moisture as well as colder temperatures. Snow levels, high at the onset of the event, dropped down to around 6000 feet before this system exited the region. Moderate, wet snow fell across most mountain areas as winds gusted up to 35 mph at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 5 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 30 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms brought severe weather hazards to the state of Utah, including flash flooding in southern and eastern Utah, and damaging winds, hail, and a tornado in Northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dirty Devil - Poison Springs Above Hanskville gauge indicated a rise from a baseline around 500 CFS to 14100 CFS, or from a gauge height of about 8 feet to about 21 feet. According to United States Geological Survey, the peak flow rate of 14100 CFS ranks as the 6th highest peak flow rate for all water years back to 1948. The peak gauge height of 21 feet ranks as the 3rd highest peak gauge height for all water years back to 1948.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms brought severe weather hazards to the state of Utah, including flash flooding in southern and eastern Utah, and damaging winds, hail, and a tornado in Northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado first touched down in North Salt Lake at Redwood Road and 900 W around 628 PM MDT. The tornado moved to the northeast through a mix of industrial and residential areas. The tornado produced EF0 damage (winds 65 to 85 MPH) to numerous trees and several homes in the Cloverdale neighborhood. The tornado continued to move off to the northeast, producing more EF0 damage to the Woods Cross Public Works building and a nearby construction site. The tornado reached peak intensity along 1100 W, producing EF1 damage (winds 86 to 110 MPH) where it uprooted a healthy mature tree and leaned a wooden power pole. The tornado continued to the north, weakening, but still producing EF0 damage before lifting off the ground.||The tornado then made a second touchdown in a field near 800 W where it produced EF0 damage to a business in the area and began moving northward along 800 W. Tree damage and minor property damage indicative of an EF0 tornado was widespread along this swath. Large diameter tree limbs (in excess of 12 inches) were broken off and deposited throughout the area. The tornado then dissipated in the area of 1500 S and Interstate 15 around 634 PM MDT.||The rating for this tornado is EF1 with winds as high as 110 MPH. The tornado had an intermittent track length of about 2 miles over 7 minutes and a max-width of approximately 350 yards with an average width of approximately 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough slowly moved inland across the western United States. Unsettled westerly flow out of this system, paired with a series of embedded disturbances, resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snow in the higher elevations. A strong cold front produced heavy snow bands, one of which sat over east-central Utah for several hours. Winds gusted up to 55 mph in the mountains and up to 40 mph in the valleys. Bitterly cold temperatures moved into the region in the wake of the trough's passage on New Year's Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 18 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with over 2 feet estimated at the La Sal Mountain Upper SNOTEL site. 4 inches fell in the town of Monticello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough dominated the western United States and kicked out a series of Pacific systems across the region. This wave train impacted eastern Utah for several days and produced heavy snow in the mountains as well as very strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 51 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial shortwave trough and then a larger open wave moved across eastern Utah. Strong upper level support, impressive moisture, widescale synoptic lift, and a strong surface cold front resulted in widespread precipitation with moderate to heavy snow accumulating in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 16 to 26 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anonymously strong trough of low pressure dug into the Great Basin. A tightened gradient aloft resulted in gusty winds at the surface, some of which exceeded high wind criteria in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport in Moab reported a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture remained trapped across the region in the wake of a departing system. This led to the development of dense fog across the Arches/Grand Flat area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Arches/Grand Flat area including at the Canyonlands Field Airport in Moab as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for a couple of hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific trough lifted across eastern Utah and brought moderate snow to many mountain ranges. Wind gusts ranged from 50 to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 5 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted from 55 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged atmospheric river event brought anomalously high moisture to eastern Utah as a series of Pacific systems lifted across the region. Heavy snow fell in the mountains resulting in hazardous holiday travel with blowing and drifting snow occurring as wind gusts exceeded 45 mph in the higher elevations. Mild southwest flow early in the event resulted in high snow levels before a cold front arrived later on.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 22 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anonymously strong trough of low pressure dug into the Great Basin. A 170 knot jetstreak resided over eastern Utah which resulted in gusty winds at the surface, some of which exceeded high wind criteria in the Grand Flat and Arches region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather station at the Canyonlands Field Airport in Moab reported a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave trough trekked across the Northern Rockies beneath a weakening ridge. This feature tapped into the already present monsoonal moisture to produce scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized flash flooding across portions of Grand County in east-central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey found evidence of a major flash flooding event in the Westwater Drainage Valley. Water was estimated to be 6 to up to 12 feet deep based on erosion of the arroyo walls. Numerous instances of dry arroyo crossings were washed out with significant transport of sediment and debris downvalley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad midlevel trough moved across eastern Utah and acted on ample residual monsoonal moisture to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area. Some storms produced gusty winds to 70 mph as well as heavy rain which led to localized debris flows in San Juan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A minor debris flow occurred at mile marker 111 on Highway 191. Rocks estimated to be the size of microwaves and other debris closed the road for a short while until it could be cleared. Radar estimated 0.50 inches of rain fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Substantial monsoon moisture remained over the area as high pressure drifted east over the Plains. As a result, widespread showers and thunderstorms developed with many storms producing heavy rain. This led to several reports of flash flooding and debris flows across portions of southeast Utah including over the Pack Creek Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain led to flash flooding in downtown Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture remained in place across the Western Slope. As a result, another round of widespread showers and thunderstorms developed across the area with many storms producing heavy rain. This led to flash flooding and debris flows over the Pack Creek Fire burn scar and south of Moab in Grand and San Juan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A six foot wall of water, boulders and debris swept over North Desert Solitaire Road within the Pack Creek Fire burn scar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along Connector Road near Ken's Lake Campground.
